Scenario.title: Oldsmar Water Treatment Plant Cybersecurity Breach

overview.description: The Oldsmar Treatment Plant was targeted by a sophisticated cyberattack involving spear phishing, remote code execution, and system configuration tampering to disrupt water treatment operations.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Disruption of public infrastructure and potential environmental harm.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2009-3953 (Suggested): Microsoft Windows Kernel 'msvjpgvcodec99.dll' Integer Overflow Vulnerability
stage.description: An email-based phishing attack targeting employees with malicious links designed to exploit a known vulnerability, gaining initial access to the network.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 253 Name: Remote Code Inclusion
stage.description: Exploitation of a remote code execution vulnerability in the SCADA system to execute malicious commands and establish a foothold within the network.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 70 Name: Try Common or Default Usernames and Passwords
stage.description: Creation of a new user account with elevated privileges to maintain persistent access to the compromised system.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 121 Name: Exploit Non-Production Interfaces
stage.description: Use of known vulnerabilities in exposed applications to escalate privileges and gain control over critical systems.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Manipulation of log files to erase evidence of the attack and evade detection mechanisms.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 678 Name: System Build Data Maliciously Altered
stage.description: Modification of chemical dosing parameters in the SCADA system to disrupt water treatment processes and potentially compromise public health.

Scenario.summary: The attack begins with a spear phishing email exploiting a known vulnerability, followed by remote code execution to gain initial access. Subsequent stages involve creating a persistence mechanism, privilege escalation through application exploitation, defense evasion by clearing logs, and finally impacting operations by altering system configurations to disrupt water treatment processes.
